subject: Large Language Models
description: Marking guidelines for LLM engineering questions covering architecture, training, fine-tuning, inference, and deployment.
importantNote: LLM questions are assessed on conceptual accuracy, mathematical correctness, implementation quality, and depth of engineering understanding. Partial credit is awarded for correct reasoning even with minor errors.

general: Conceptual Accuracy: Are the LLM concepts, mechanisms, and terminology used correctly?
Mathematical Correctness: Are formulas, derivations, and numerical calculations accurate?
Implementation Quality: Is code correct, clean, and following ML engineering conventions?
Engineering Depth: Does the answer demonstrate practical understanding beyond surface-level definitions?
Clarity: Are explanations clear, precise, and well-structured?
Critical Thinking: Does the answer address trade-offs, limitations, and real-world considerations?

rules.condition: Correct concept with minor mathematical error (e.g. off-by-one in formula)
Correct formula but wrong numerical answer due to arithmetic error
Partially correct explanation missing one key component
rules.marksAwarded: 50-70% depending on which component is missing

rules.condition: Code that is conceptually correct but has a minor bug (e.g. wrong dimension)
Correct answer without justification or explanation
rules.marksAwarded: 40-60% — marks require reasoning, not just the answer

rules.condition: Demonstrates understanding of trade-offs and limitations
rules.marksAwarded: Full marks possible even if minor factual error present

rules.condition: Answer for a different but related concept (e.g. MQA instead of GQA)

expectations: Correctly define Q, K, V in attention and explain the scaled dot-product formula
Distinguish encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder architectures with examples
Explain causal vs masked language modelling objectives
Describe BPE tokenisation at an algorithmic level
Understand residual connections and layer normalisation roles in Transformer blocks
commonErrors: Confusing Q, K, V roles — especially in cross-attention vs self-attention
Omitting the sqrt(d_k) scaling factor from the attention formula
Stating that encoder-only models are autoregressive (they are not)
Mixing up GPT (decoder-only) with BERT (encoder-only) capabilities

expectations: Implement scaled dot-product attention and multi-head attention from scratch
Correctly implement LoRA with proper initialisation (B=0) and scaling
Explain RLHF three-phase pipeline and why KL penalty is necessary
Design and implement a basic RAG retrieval pipeline with cosine similarity
Calculate KV cache memory for a given model configuration
Apply chain-of-thought and compare zero-shot vs few-shot prompting strategies
commonErrors: Initialising LoRA's B matrix with random values instead of zeros
Forgetting the output projection W_O in multi-head attention
Treating reward model score as ground truth in RLHF discussion
Not normalising embeddings before cosine similarity computation
Confusing training FLOPs with inference FLOPs in scaling law calculations

expectations: Derive Chinchilla compute-optimal N and D from a given FLOP budget with correct formula
Implement DPO loss with correct log-ratio computation and beta scaling
Design a multi-step LLM agent loop with tool schemas, execution, and result injection
Explain GQA/MQA and their impact on KV cache memory at inference time
Analyse hallucination causes and propose targeted pipeline fixes
Compare quantisation methods (INT4, INT8, fp16) with accuracy-efficiency trade-offs
commonErrors: Using N = C/6 instead of N = C/120 in Chinchilla formula (forgetting the 20 tokens per param factor)
DPO loss: applying beta to only one ratio term instead of the difference
Agent implementation: not returning tool results back to LLM for final response generation
Confusing quantisation of weights vs activations in INT4/GPTQ discussion
Over-simplifying hallucination: treating it as a single phenomenon rather than distinguishing intrinsic/extrinsic/sycophantic

criteria.description: All 4 parts fully correct: exact attention formula with Q/K/V definitions, correct scaling justification, correct d_head=64 and tensor shapes, correct two sub-layers with pre/post-norm explained, correct architecture comparison with appropriate examples
3 of 4 parts fully correct; one part has minor omission (e.g. forgets output projection W_O, or misses exposure bias in part (a))
2 of 4 parts correct; common gap: attention formula correct but missing scaling justification; or architecture types named correctly but without examples or clear capability distinction
1-2 parts partially correct; demonstrates basic familiarity but significant conceptual gaps (e.g. confuses encoder and decoder capabilities)
Fundamental misunderstanding of attention mechanism or Transformer architecture

All 4 parts fully correct: CLM objective maximisation stated, teacher forcing and exposure bias correctly described, CE=-log(0.02)≈3.91 and PPL=50 calculated, BPE algorithm and byte-level distinction correct, tokenisation efficiency implications (quadratic attention, context utilisation) clearly explained
3 of 4 parts correct; common error: perplexity = cross-entropy (not exp), or missing exposure bias in part (a)
2 of 4 correct; may have CE/PPL calculation error, or BPE described only at high level without byte-level distinction
1-2 partially correct; CLM objective stated informally without mathematical notation; byte-level BPE confused with standard BPE
Fundamental misunderstanding of training objectives or tokenisation

LoRA: correct implementation with B=0 init, correct scaling, merge_weights(), correct param count (131,072). RLHF: all 3 phases with correct input/objective/output, KL penalty explained as anti-reward-hacking. DPO: correct loss formula, insightful one-sentence explanation linking to implicit reward model
LoRA mostly correct but missing merge_weights() or incorrect scaling. RLHF phases described but one phase's objective imprecise. DPO formula correct but explanation superficial
LoRA works but B initialised randomly (significant error). RLHF 2 of 3 phases correctly described. DPO formula has minor error but understanding shown
LoRA: general idea but not implementable. RLHF: only SFT and RM described, PPO/KL missing. DPO: formula attempted but incorrect
Minimal correct content; significant conceptual errors in fine-tuning fundamentals
No meaningful attempt

Chunking calculation correct with overlap reasoning. Retriever: normalisation, cosine similarity via dot product, correct top-k logic. Dense vs sparse comparison: all 3 dimensions with accurate characterisation. Hybrid: meaningful score combination formula. Hallucination: 3 distinct causes with specific, actionable fixes (not vague 'improve the model')
3 of 4 parts fully correct; common gap: retriever missing normalisation, or hallucination causes identified but fixes too vague
2 of 4 parts correct; retriever implemented but uses Euclidean distance instead of cosine; OR chunking formula correct but overlap rationale missing
Chunking calculation attempt; basic retriever without normalisation; dense vs sparse superficially compared
Minimal understanding of RAG pipeline components

All 4 parts excellent: KV cache formula correct with all factors (2×layers×kv_heads×d_head×seq×bytes), correct ≈5GB result. INT4: clear weight/activation distinction, 4x compression, appropriate accuracy vs memory trade-off analysis. Chinchilla: correct N*=C/120≈833M, D*≈16.7B, GPT-3 correctly identified as 67x over-sized relative to compute-optimal. Agent: working tool schemas, correct tool selection logic, result injection into message history
3 of 4 parts fully correct; KV cache may have minor arithmetic error; OR Chinchilla uses C/6 instead of C/120 (missing 20 tokens factor); OR agent missing result-injection step
2-3 parts correct; Chinchilla formula wrong (using N=C/6); KV cache formula missing one factor; agent missing tool execution or final response generation
Partial credit across multiple parts; general understanding of concepts but significant execution errors
One part partially correct; fundamental gaps in inference optimisation or scaling law understanding
No meaningful attempt
